summary: Gruber dissects Phil Spencer's criticism of Apple's DMA compliance proposals, exposing what he sees as blatant hypocrisy. Spencer argues iOS should be open to alternative storefronts like Windows is, but Xbox — his own platform — is equally or more closed than iOS. Gruber notes that Spencer conspicuously compares iOS to Windows rather than to Xbox, likely because Xbox's own policies mirror Apple's. The post also briefly covers Microsoft's broader strategy of bringing Xbox exclusives to rival consoles, comparing it to Warner Bros. putting content on Netflix. Gruber's central argument is that Spencer has no credibility demanding Apple open its platform when he could unilaterally open Xbox first and chooses not to.
key_points: Phil Spencer criticizes Apple's DMA compliance as not going far enough, while Xbox operates under similarly or more restrictive rules than iOS
Spencer strategically compares iOS to Windows rather than Xbox, because the Xbox comparison would undermine his entire argument
Microsoft's broader gaming strategy of releasing exclusives on rival platforms signals a second-fiddle market position similar to Warner Bros. putting content on Netflix
iOS became the largest gaming platform under its current closed rules — it didn't become popular and then close down
Xbox president Sarah Bond's retweet of Spotify CEO Daniel Ek's anti-Apple diatribe revealed Microsoft's actual stance more than her diplomatic tweet did
money_quotes: Why doesn't Xbox work like Windows works? There's no Steam or Epic Games Store or GOG on iOS. But there's no Steam or Epic Games Store or GOG on Xbox.

How in the world does Spencer think Apple should be forced by government regulators to open their platform to these alternative stores when he could snap his fingers and open his company's own platform, Xbox, to these same stores?

It's ridiculous for the CEO of Xbox to argue that iOS should have similar rules and policies to Windows, when Xbox — another platform from the same company — has rules that are, if anything, more restrictive and exclusive than iOS.

It would be ludicrous for Tim Cook or Eddy Cue or Phil Schiller to argue that Xbox should have the same rules as Windows — but they're not making that argument. Spencer is. And he's in charge of Xbox!
If he thinks iOS should open up to zero-royalty, zero-fee native app distribution, open up Xbox first. Put your money where your mouth is.
More than any of the words in her own tweet, what conveyed Microsoft's actual stance towards Apple's proposals is the fact that her tweet was a retweet of Spotify CEO Daniel Ek's diatribe.
Second-fiddle bends to the market leader.
key_insight: Phil Spencer's demand that Apple open iOS to alternative storefronts is pure hypocrisy when Xbox — his own platform — operates under equally or more restrictive rules that he could change unilaterally.
